Although shells are cheaper and more numerous than torpedoes, that economy does not rescue the overall design.
segments.question: If HMS M1 and M2 had survived, which cruiser-submarine design would have received further development?
segments.answerShort: Neither; Dr. Clarke regards both as expressions of a fundamentally flawed cruiser-submarine concept.

evidence.note: The answer explains the intended cruiser-submarine mission and why its mixed surface and submerged roles do not work well.

segments.title: Enterprise, Roberts, and the original frigates anchor US naval naming
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke favors keeping several storied names, including those of the six original frigates, continuously represented in the fleet.
segments.body: Enterprise is accepted as an obvious name for continued service. Dr. Clarke adds Samuel B. Roberts and Constellation to the list. He then broadens the principle to the names of the United States Navy's six original frigates. The choices connect modern commissioning decisions with famous service histories and the institutional origins of the navy.
segments.question: Which United States Navy names should always have a ship in commission?
segments.answerShort: Enterprise, Samuel B. Roberts, Constellation, and the names of the six original frigates are strong candidates.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke lists Enterprise, Samuel B. Roberts, Constellation, and the six original frigates.

segments.title: Foreknowledge of U-boat numbers accelerates British escorts
segments.summary: Knowing Germany's eventual wartime submarine totals would push Britain toward earlier sloop and Flower-class corvette construction.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke notes that the scenario grants Britain knowledge Germany itself did not yet possess, since the cited totals were wartime outcomes rather than a fixed early plan. With that warning, the likely response is to accelerate escorts already suited to anti-submarine work. Earlier Flower-class corvettes and sloops would expand convoy protection before the U-boat campaign reached full strength.
segments.question: What would Britain do if it knew early that Germany would build hundreds of Type VII, Type IX, and Type II U-boats?
segments.answerShort: It would probably begin building more sloops and Flower-class corvettes earlier, though that knowledge would amount to foreseeing Germany's later wartime production.

evidence.note: The answer distinguishes future production totals from contemporary German plans and identifies earlier British escort construction.

segments.title: The Armistice ended the case for more R-class submarines
segments.summary: The Royal Navy stopped at twelve R-class boats because the war ended and postwar design study offered more value than continued production.
segments.body: The R class was built for an immediate First World War requirement, and the Armistice removed the opponent and mission that justified more boats. Dr. Clarke argues that the navy could instead digest wartime engineering and operational experience before producing a successor generation. The later U class is suggested as a closer spiritual continuation than the S class.
segments.question: Why did the Royal Navy build only twelve R-class diesel-electric hunter-killer submarines?
segments.answerShort: The war was ending, so there was no need for more; the navy could study wartime lessons and design a better next generation instead.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke ties the production total to the end of the war and the opportunity to incorporate accumulated submarine lessons.

segments.title: A larger inherited submarine fleet compounds interwar experience
segments.summary: More inherited First World War submarines would give the Royal Navy a larger operating base and feed additional experience into later designs.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke cautions that the counterfactual needs clearer assumptions about which submarines transfer and when. In general, a larger fleet would support more operations, produce more institutional experience, and encourage further construction. Those lessons would then shape the next design generation rather than leaving development unchanged.
segments.question: How would Royal Navy submarine development change if it inherited all available prewar and wartime submarines outside a treaty system?
segments.answerShort: A larger fleet would generate more operating experience and likely more construction, with those lessons feeding into subsequent designs.

evidence.note: The answer requests clearer assumptions, then describes how a larger fleet would compound experience and influence later designs.
